Organic Chemistry I Lectures CHEM-C 341, Fall 2020
Week 14 Lecture Slides – Week of 11/30/2020
Instructor: Kasey J. Clear

Substitution vs Elimination Reactions
Elimination reaction = loss of HX from an alkyl halide (or other leaving group) to leave an alkene
2 Mechanisms: E2 and E1

Role of the nucleophile in elimination reactions
Nucleophiles that are strong bases promote elimination by E2 mechanism

Role of the nucleophile in elimination reactions
Table of common nucleophile structures, names, and relative strength and basicity
Strong nucleophiles | Weak(ish) bases: 
I- (iodide), 
N3- (azide), 
-CN (cyanide, makes nitriles), 
:PR3 (phosphine, makes phosphonium), 
RS-/HS- (thiolate/sulfide)

Strong bases: 
RO-/HO- (alkoxide/hydroxide), 
RC≡C- (acetylide), 
NH2- (amide, makes amines),
Moderate  nucleophiles | Br-/Cl- (bromide/chloride), 
RNH2/NH3 (amine/ammonia), 
RSH (thiol), 
RCOO- (carboxylate)
Weak nucleophiles | F- (fluoride), 
RCOOH (carboxylic acid), 
ROH/H2O (alcohol/water)

Role of the nucleophile in elimination reactions
Elimination reactions are most favored when the competing substitution reaction is slow
= bulky, non-nucleophilic base

Role of temperature in elimination reactions
Elimination reactions are more favored with increasing temperature

Role of leaving group in elimination reactions
Leaving group is not very important as long as it is a good leaving group
Good leaving groups
Substrate | Leaving group
R-Cl
R-Br
R-I
R-OSO2R’
R-+OH2
R-+OHR’
R-+NR’3 | Cl- (chloride)
Br- (bromide)
I- (iodide)
R’SO3- (sulfonate)
H2O (water)
R’OH (alcohol)
NR’3 (amine)
Practice

Hydrogenation is a chemical reaction that transforms an alkene into an alkane  uses H 2 gas and a catalyst ( Pd /C)
3-methyl-1-butene
2-methyl-1-butene
2-methyl-2-butene
Heat of hydrogenation = amount of energy stored in an  			          alkene π bond
More energy in bond = ___________ stable bond
Hydrogenation of alkenes
+ 127 kJ/ mol
+ 119 kJ/ mol
+ 113 kJ/ mol

1) More highly substituted alkenes are more stable:
tetrasubstituted > trisubstituted > disubstituted > monosubstituted
2) trans alkenes are more stable than cis alkenes cis            <             trans
Trends in alkene stability

Which alkene is the most stable?
Which alkene is the least stable?
Which alkene has the largest heat of hydrogenation?

Most important E1 reaction: alcohol dehydration

Organic Chemistry I Lectures CHEM-C 341, Fall 2020
Week 14 Lecture Slides – Week of 11/30/2020
Instructor: Kasey J. Clear

Anti- periplanar transition state in E2
Reminder: E2 reactions require strong base
E2 reactions are fastest when leaving group and adjacent hydrogen are anti

Anti- periplanar transition state in E2
E2 reactions can even be stereospecific (one reactant stereoisomer gives a particular product stereoisomer)

E2 reactions on substituted cyclohexane
Leaving group must be axial and there must be an anti- periplanar hydrogen to remove

Regioselectivity of E2 reactions & influence of base
Bulky bases remove the most accessible hydrogen
Small bases usually produce the more stable alkene

Another mechanism: E1cB
Possible when there are anion stabilizing groups:
Putting it all together

Substitution or Elimination?
1° alkyl halide + strong base/nucleophile: substitution (S N 2) products
2° alkyl halide + strong base/nucleophile: mostly E2 products
3°/hindered alkyl halide + strong base: elimination (E2) products
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2 ways to favor E2 products:
1) Bulky base/nucleophile
2) Higher temperature (eliminations have more favorable Δ S)
2° alkyl halide + strong nucleophile (weak base): mostly S N 2

1° alkyl halide + weak base/nucleophile: no reaction
2° or 3° alkyl halide + weak base/nucleophile: mixture of E1 and S N 1 --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Which of the alkyl halides reacts fastest in an… S N 2 reaction?				S N 1 reaction? E2 reaction?				E1 reaction?

What are the products of the reaction of 2-bromobutane with sodium methoxide?
What are the products of the reaction of 1-bromobutane with sodium methoxide?
What are the products of the reaction of 2-bromo-2-methylbutane with sodium methoxide?
